title: Proclus' Hymns — Essays, Translations, Commentary (R.M. van den Berg)
subtitle: The Brill monograph on Proclus' seven hymns as theurgic practice — prayer as epistrophē, symbols as Mātṛkā activation

summary.one_line: van den Berg's 2001 Brill monograph demonstrating that Proclus' hymns are not devotional poetry but theurgic technology — activating the soul's innate connection to the divine through symphonetic p...
summary.scope: Covers the theory behind theurgy (Iamblichus/Proclus), the hymns as theurgy in practice, Proclus' theory of prayer as epistrophē, the heliotrope argument, symbols as divine seeds, and the seven hymns translated with full commentary.

sources.label: R.M. van den Berg — Proclus' Hymns: Essays, Translations, Commentary (Brill, 2001). Philosophia Antiqua Vol. XC. Full text in corpus at /root/projects/blog/proclushymns (20,696 lines).

body.text: The heliotrope passages (De Sacrificio 148): 'Since everything prays according to its own order and celebrates in hymns the leaders of the whole series — noerically, with words, physically, or perceptibly — the heliotrope too moves to the extent that it is flexible. If someone would be capable of hearing it hitting the air as it turns around, he would observe that by means of this sound it renders to the King a kind of hymn that a plant is capable of singing.' The plant's turning IS the hymn. The practitioner's chant IS the same process made conscious. The heliotrope does not ASK for sunlight — it TURNS toward the sun. The turning IS the hymn.

The Moirai (Fates) provide the model: 'The act of singing hymns itself as such makes it clear that their noeric activity is turned towards their superior causes, since hymns are sung about superior gods, not about those who are inferior.' Prayer IS epistrophē — the soul's return to its source. The hymn is not a request but a REVERSION. The soul turns toward the god, and in turning, remembers its own divine nature. This IS pratyabhijñā — recognition through the act of invocation.

The divine names in the hymns are symbola — tokens placed in the soul by the gods. They are not arbitrary signs but phoneme sequences that are structurally connected to the god. When chanted, they activate the corresponding Mātṛkā circuit. The hymn functions as a sonic yantra: the name IS the deity in sound-form. The practitioner does not call a distant god — they become the god by vibrating the god's name through their own nāḍīs.

gaps: Full translation of all seven hymns needed; detailed comparison with Tantraloka Mātṛkā theory

issues.title: Full hymn translations not extracted
issues.description: The seven hymns with van den Berg's commentary are in the 20,696-line text but not extracted into the RO.

versions.summary: Initial RO for van den Berg's Proclus' Hymns. 3 passages covering the heliotrope hymn, prayer as epistrophē, and symbols as Mātṛkā.

timeline.event: RO created from van den Berg's Brill monograph. The Proclus hymns are now formally mapped to Mātṛkā theory.
